q: How many stones did David take from the brook to face Goliath?
why: David chose five smooth stones from the brook, though he needed only one to bring Goliath down.

q: Which book comes right after the Gospels?
why: Acts continues Luke's account, telling how the church spread from Jerusalem to Rome.

q: On which island was Paul shipwrecked on his voyage to Rome?
why: After fourteen days of storm the ship ran aground on Malta, where the islanders showed unusual kindness.

q: How many days and nights was Jonah in the belly of the fish?
why: God prepared a great fish to hold Jonah for three days and nights, giving him time to repent and pray.

q: How many times did Jesus say Peter would deny him before the rooster crowed?
why: Jesus told Peter he would deny him three times that very night, a prediction that came true even as Peter wept over it.

q: Which of these is NOT one of the twelve apostles listed in Matthew?
why: Barnabas was an important early church leader and Paul's companion, but he was not one of the original twelve apostles.

q: What did God create on the very first day, according to Genesis?
why: Before the sun or stars existed, God spoke light into being and separated it from darkness on day one.

q: How many cubits wide was Noah's ark?
why: God gave Noah exact measurements: three hundred cubits long, fifty wide, and thirty high, a design built to endure the flood.

q: For how many days and nights did it rain during the flood?
why: The rain fell for forty days and nights, and the waters kept rising for months afterward before receding.

q: What did the Nile's water turn into during the first plague on Egypt?
why: Aaron struck the river with his staff and it turned to blood, the first of ten plagues that led to Israel's release.

q: How many commandments did God write on the stone tablets?
why: After forty days on the mountain, Moses came down with the Ten Commandments, the heart of the covenant law.

q: What was the secret of Samson's great strength?
why: As a Nazirite from birth, Samson's strength was tied to his vow never to cut his hair; Delilah had it shaved off to betray him.

q: How tall was the Philistine champion Goliath, in cubits?
why: Goliath stood over nine feet tall by most reckonings, an intimidating giant that made David's faith in God all the more remarkable.

q: When God offered Solomon anything he wanted, what did he ask for?
why: Solomon asked for wisdom to govern well rather than wealth or power, and God was so pleased he gave him riches too.

q: How did Elijah leave the earth?
choices: In a chariot of fire, by a whirlwind
why: Elijah was taken up to heaven in a whirlwind, with a chariot and horses of fire appearing before Elisha's eyes.

q: What did God send to protect Daniel when he was thrown into the lions' den?
choices: An angel who shut the lions' mouths
why: Daniel testified that God sent his angel to shut the lions' mouths because he was found innocent before God.

q: How much hotter was the furnace made for Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego?
why: Nebuchadnezzar's fury led him to heat the furnace seven times more than usual, yet the three men walked out unharmed.

q: In which town was Jesus born?
why: Jesus was born in Bethlehem of Judea, fulfilling the prophecy that a ruler would come from that small town.

q: What gifts did the wise men bring to the young Jesus?
why: The wise men opened their treasures and offered gifts fit for a king, a priest, and one destined to suffer.

q: What appeared over Jesus as he came up from being baptized?
why: The Spirit of God descended like a dove as a voice from heaven declared Jesus his beloved Son.

q: How many days did Jesus fast in the wilderness before being tempted?
why: Jesus fasted forty days and nights, echoing Israel's forty years, and answered every temptation with Scripture.

q: How many loaves and fish fed the five thousand?
why: A boy's small lunch became more than enough when Jesus blessed and multiplied it for the crowd.

q: Where did Jesus turn water into wine?
why: At a wedding in Cana, Jesus performed his first recorded miracle, turning ordinary water into fine wine.

q: How many days had Lazarus been in the tomb when Jesus arrived?
why: Martha noted there would already be a stench after four days, making Jesus' raising of Lazarus all the more astonishing.

q: For how many pieces of silver did Judas agree to betray Jesus?
why: The chief priests weighed out thirty pieces of silver, the price of a slave, to pay Judas for his betrayal.

q: What did Judas do with the thirty pieces of silver after Jesus was condemned?
choices: Threw it down in the temple
why: Overcome with remorse, Judas threw the silver into the temple before going away and hanging himself.

q: What appeared over the disciples' heads at Pentecost?
why: Tongues like fire rested on each believer as the Holy Spirit filled them and they began to speak in other languages.

q: What happened to Saul on the road to Damascus?
choices: A light blinded him and he heard Jesus' voice
why: A sudden light from heaven struck Saul down, and Jesus' voice confronted him, turning history's fiercest persecutor into its greatest missionary.

q: Who was the first Christian martyr, stoned for his faith?
why: As he was stoned, Stephen prayed for his killers' forgiveness, echoing Jesus' own words from the cross.

q: "Yahweh is my shepherd; I shall lack nothing" opens which psalm?
why: Psalm 23 pictures God's tender care with the image of a shepherd who provides, guides, and protects his flock.

q: According to Proverbs, what is the beginning of knowledge?
why: Proverbs teaches that true wisdom starts with reverence for God, not merely with cleverness or learning.

q: Which of these is listed as a fruit of the Spirit in Galatians?
why: Paul lists love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faith, gentleness, and self-control as the Spirit's fruit growing in a believer's life.

q: Paul tells believers to put on what in Ephesians 6?
why: Paul describes spiritual armor -- truth, righteousness, faith, and the Spirit's sword -- to help believers stand firm against evil.

q: How many years did Israel wander in the wilderness?
why: Because the people distrusted God's promise after the spies' report, that generation wandered forty years before entering the land.

q: On which day did the walls of Jericho fall?
why: Israel marched around Jericho once a day for six days, then seven times on the seventh day, and the walls collapsed at the trumpet blast.

q: Where did Naomi and her family move to because of a famine?
why: Naomi's family left Bethlehem for Moab during a famine, where her sons married Moabite women including Ruth.

q: Whom did Esther replace as queen?
why: After Queen Vashti was deposed for refusing the king's summons, Esther was chosen queen and later used her position to save her people.

q: After his suffering, how much did God give Job compared to before?
why: Yahweh restored Job's fortunes and gave him twice what he had before, a picture of God's abundant compassion after suffering.

q: What name does Isaiah's prophecy give to the child born of a virgin?
why: Immanuel means 'God with us,' a name pointing forward to Jesus' birth centuries later.

q: What object lesson did God give Jeremiah at the potter's house?
why: Watching the potter rework marred clay taught Jeremiah that God can reshape his people according to his purposes.

q: What did Ezekiel see filling the valley in his vision?
why: God showed Ezekiel a valley of dry bones coming to life, a picture of hope for Israel's restoration.

q: What object did God show Amos to picture Israel's crookedness?
why: A plumb line shows whether a wall stands straight; God used it to show how far Israel had drifted from what was right.

q: According to Micah, from which small town would Israel's ruler come?
why: Micah named Bethlehem centuries before Jesus' birth there, a promise that God keeps his word even through the smallest places.

q: In Malachi, what does God invite the people to test him on?
why: God promises to pour out abundant blessing on those who trust him enough to bring their full tithe into the storehouse.

q: Which two Old Testament figures appeared with Jesus at the Transfiguration?
why: Moses represented the Law and Elijah the Prophets, both appearing with Jesus to show he fulfills everything they pointed to.

q: In the parable of the prodigal son, what does the father do when he sees his son returning?
choices: Runs to him and embraces him
why: While the son was still far off, the father ran to him, showing how eagerly God welcomes anyone who turns back home.

q: In the parable of the Good Samaritan, on which road was the traveler attacked?
why: A Samaritan, despised by many Jews of the day, was the one who stopped to show mercy to the wounded traveler.

q: What did Thomas need to see before he believed Jesus had risen?
choices: The nail marks in Jesus' hands
why: Jesus graciously showed Thomas his wounds, and Thomas responded with the confession, 'My Lord and my God!'

q: What command did Jesus give his disciples just before ascending to heaven?
why: The Great Commission sends believers to teach and baptize people of every nation, with the promise of Jesus' presence always.

q: What did God provide instead of Isaac for Abraham's sacrifice?
choices: A ram caught in a thicket
why: At the last moment God provided a ram, showing that he himself would provide the sacrifice his people needed.

q: What new name did God give Jacob after he wrestled with him?
why: The name Israel marks Jacob as one who struggled with God and with people and prevailed, becoming the name of a whole nation.

q: What special item did Jacob give Joseph that made his brothers jealous?
why: Jacob's obvious favoritism toward Joseph, shown through this special coat, stirred deep jealousy among his brothers.

q: For how many pieces of silver was Joseph sold to the Ishmaelite traders?
why: Joseph's brothers sold him for twenty pieces of silver, a betrayal God later turned toward saving many lives.

q: In what did baby Moses' mother place him to hide him from Pharaoh?
choices: A wooden box buried in sand
why: Moses' mother placed him in a papyrus basket coated with tar and set it among the reeds, trusting God with her son's life.

q: Where did God first speak to Moses out of fire?
why: The bush burned but was not consumed, a striking sign that drew Moses near before God called him to lead Israel out of Egypt.

q: How did Israel cross the Red Sea?
choices: On dry ground through a divided sea
why: A strong east wind drove the sea back all night, and Israel walked through on dry ground with walls of water on either side.

q: What food did God provide for Israel each morning in the wilderness?
why: Manna appeared like frost on the ground each morning, bread from heaven that sustained Israel through forty years of wandering.

q: What idol did the Israelites make while Moses was on the mountain?
why: While Moses received God's law, the impatient people melted their gold into a calf to worship, a grievous betrayal of the covenant.

q: Which two spies gave a faithful report and trusted God could take the land?
why: While ten spies spread fear, Caleb and Joshua urged Israel to trust God's power to give them the promised land.

q: What unusual thing did Balaam's donkey do on the road?
why: Yahweh opened the donkey's mouth to rebuke Balaam, a strange and humbling sign meant to stop him in his tracks.

q: What did Rahab hang from her window as a sign for the Israelite spies?
why: The scarlet cord marked Rahab's house for protection when Jericho fell, because she had hidden and helped Israel's spies.

q: Who was the prophetess and judge who led Israel alongside Barak?
why: Deborah judged Israel wisely and courageously led the army to victory over their oppressors alongside Barak.

q: What sign did Gideon ask God for using a wool fleece?
choices: Dew on the fleece but dry ground
why: Gideon asked for reassurance through the fleece test, and God patiently confirmed his call not once but twice.

q: How many men did God use to defeat the Midianites under Gideon?
why: God reduced Gideon's army to just three hundred men so that Israel would know the victory came from him, not their own strength.

q: What name did Hannah give the son God gave her after years of prayer?
why: Hannah named her long-awaited son Samuel, meaning 'asked of God,' and dedicated him to the Lord's service.

q: What kind of covenant did Jonathan make with David?
why: Jonathan loved David as his own soul and made a covenant with him, a friendship that held even when it cost Jonathan dearly.

q: When David had the chance to kill Saul in a cave, what did he do instead?
choices: Cut off a piece of his robe
why: David spared Saul's life and only cut off the edge of his robe, refusing to harm the Lord's anointed even when urged to.

q: Whose wife was Bathsheba before David took her?
why: David's sin against Uriah and Bathsheba led to deep consequences, yet also to David's genuine repentance recorded in Psalm 51.

q: What happened to Absalom that led to his death while fleeing battle?
choices: His hair caught in a tree branch
He was thrown from his horse
why: Riding under a great oak, Absalom's head was caught in its branches, leaving him helpless when Joab's men found him.

q: How many years did it take Solomon to build the temple?
why: Solomon spent seven years constructing the temple in Jerusalem, a magnificent house built for the worship of God.

q: How did Solomon expose the real mother in the dispute over a baby?
choices: He offered to divide the child in two
why: The true mother begged for the baby's life to be spared even if it meant losing him, revealing her identity through her love.

q: On Mount Carmel, how many prophets of Baal did Elijah confront?
why: Elijah stood alone against four hundred fifty prophets of Baal, and God answered with fire to prove he alone is God.

q: What creature fed Elijah bread and meat by the brook Cherith?
why: God used ravens, unlikely providers, to bring Elijah food morning and evening while he hid from Ahab.

q: What did Elisha ask Elijah for before he was taken up to heaven?
choices: A double portion of his spirit
why: Elisha asked for a double portion of Elijah's spirit so he could carry on the prophetic ministry with even greater impact.

q: How many times did Naaman have to dip in the Jordan to be healed of leprosy?
why: Naaman's pride nearly kept him from obeying, but washing seven times in the Jordan as Elisha instructed brought complete healing.

q: What did Hilkiah the priest discover while the temple was being repaired?
why: Finding the long-neglected Book of the Law sparked King Josiah's zealous reforms to turn the nation back to God.

q: In how many days did Nehemiah and the people rebuild Jerusalem's wall?
why: Despite fierce opposition, the wall was finished in just fifty-two days, a testament to the people's unity and God's help.

q: What did Ezra do when he gathered the people at the Water Gate?
choices: Read the Book of the Law to them
why: Ezra read the law aloud from morning until midday, and the people listened attentively as it was explained to them.

q: According to Isaiah 53, what happened to the suffering servant?
choices: He was pierced for our transgressions
why: Isaiah's prophecy describes a servant crushed and pierced so that others could be healed, pointing ahead to Jesus' suffering for us.

q: Where was Jeremiah thrown by officials who wanted him silenced?
why: Jeremiah sank into the mire of an empty cistern for speaking hard truths, yet God preserved his life through a compassionate official.

q: What strange vision did Ezekiel see involving wheels?
choices: A wheel within a wheel among living creatures
why: Ezekiel's vision of wheels within wheels pictured God's presence moving with perfect, unrestricted freedom.

q: What words appeared on the wall during Belshazzar's feast?
why: Daniel interpreted the mysterious writing as God's verdict that Belshazzar's kingdom had been weighed and found wanting.

q: Whom did God tell Hosea to marry as a picture of Israel's unfaithfulness?
why: Hosea's difficult marriage to Gomer became a living picture of God's persistent love for an unfaithful people.

q: In Joel's prophecy, on whom would God's Spirit be poured out?
choices: All flesh, sons and daughters alike
why: Joel promised a day when God's Spirit would come on everyone, a promise Peter announced was being fulfilled at Pentecost.

q: Obadiah, the shortest book of the Old Testament, pronounces judgment on which nation?
why: Obadiah condemns Edom's pride and cruelty toward Israel, showing that God holds even Israel's relatives accountable.

q: According to Habakkuk, how will the righteous live?
why: Habakkuk's declaration that the righteous will live by faith became a cornerstone teaching quoted throughout the New Testament.

q: What day does Zephaniah warn is near and coming quickly?
why: Zephaniah's urgent warning about the coming day of the Lord calls people to repentance before judgment arrives.

q: What did Haggai urge the returned exiles to rebuild?
why: Haggai challenged the people to stop neglecting God's house while comfortable in their own, and rebuilding began soon after.

q: How does Zechariah say Jerusalem's king would arrive?
why: Zechariah's prophecy of a humble king riding a donkey was fulfilled when Jesus entered Jerusalem to shouts of praise.

q: Who does Jesus say is blessed first in the Beatitudes?
why: Jesus begins his most famous sermon by blessing those who recognize their spiritual need, not those who feel self-sufficient.

q: How does the Lord's Prayer begin?
why: Jesus taught his disciples to pray simply and intimately, addressing God as a loving Father.

q: How did Jesus come to his disciples' boat during a night storm?
why: Jesus walked on the sea toward his frightened disciples, showing his authority even over wind and waves.

q: What happened to Peter after he stepped out of the boat to walk to Jesus?
choices: He walked easily the whole way
He began to sink when he saw the wind
why: Peter's fear of the wind pulled his eyes off Jesus, and he began to sink until Jesus reached out and caught him.

q: What did Jesus say to calm the storm on the sea?
why: With a word, Jesus rebuked the wind and waves, and the sea became perfectly calm, amazing his disciples.

q: What Aramaic words did Jesus use to raise Jairus's daughter?
why: Jesus took the girl's hand and said 'Talitha cumi,' meaning 'Girl, get up,' and she rose immediately, healthy and alive.

q: For how many years had the woman suffered a bleeding disorder before touching Jesus' cloak?
why: After twelve years of suffering and failed treatments, she was healed the instant she touched Jesus' garment in faith.

q: What did the crowds wave and cry out as Jesus entered Jerusalem?
why: The crowd welcomed Jesus as a king, waving palm branches and shouting praises, though many misunderstood what kind of king he was.

q: What did Jesus institute at the Last Supper with bread and a cup?
choices: The Lord's Supper, his body and blood
why: Jesus gave the bread and cup new meaning as symbols of his body and blood, given for his followers to remember him by.

q: What unusual detail does Luke record about Jesus' agony in Gethsemane?
choices: His sweat became like drops of blood
why: Luke describes the intensity of Jesus' anguish in prayer, sweating drops like blood as he faced the cross ahead.

q: How many criminals were crucified alongside Jesus?
why: Jesus was crucified between two criminals, one on each side, fulfilling the prophecy that he would be numbered with transgressors.

q: What did Jesus pray for those who crucified him?
why: Even in his suffering, Jesus prayed for the forgiveness of those putting him to death, modeling the mercy he taught.

q: On what day did the women find Jesus' tomb empty?
choices: The first day of the week
The last day of the feast
why: Early on the first day of the week, the women arrived with spices and found the stone rolled away and the tomb empty.

q: To which village were two disciples walking when the risen Jesus joined them?
why: Jesus walked with two disciples toward Emmaus, opening the Scriptures to them before they recognized him at the table.

q: What was John the Baptist's diet in the wilderness?
why: John's simple, wild diet matched his call to a stark, prophetic life devoted entirely to preparing the way for Jesus.

q: What did Zacchaeus climb to see Jesus passing by?
why: Being short and eager to see Jesus, Zacchaeus climbed a tree, and Jesus surprised him by inviting himself to his house.

q: When did Nicodemus come to speak with Jesus?
why: Nicodemus, a religious leader, came secretly by night to ask Jesus about being born again, showing his sincere but cautious search.

q: Where did Jesus meet the Samaritan woman and ask her for a drink?
why: Tired from his journey, Jesus sat at Jacob's well and crossed cultural boundaries to offer a Samaritan woman living water.

q: What sin did Ananias and Sapphira commit that led to their deaths?
choices: Lying about the price of land they sold
why: Ananias and Sapphira kept back part of the money while claiming to give it all, and their deceit toward God had severe consequences.

q: Whom did Philip meet and baptize on the desert road, reading Isaiah?
why: Philip explained that Isaiah's suffering servant was Jesus, and the Ethiopian official believed and was baptized right there.

q: Whose household received the Holy Spirit in Acts 10, showing Peter that the good news was for Gentiles too?
why: Cornelius, a devout Roman centurion, and his household received the Holy Spirit while Peter was still speaking -- convincing proof that God welcomed Gentiles into the faith.

q: How was Peter freed from prison in Acts 12?
why: An angel appeared, loosed Peter's chains, and led him past the guards while the church was praying earnestly for him.

q: What happened while Paul and Silas were singing hymns in the Philippian jail?
choices: A great earthquake shook the prison open
why: God shook the prison's foundations and loosened every prisoner's chains, leading the jailer and his family to believe.

q: What issue did the Council of Jerusalem in Acts 15 mainly address?
choices: Whether Gentiles must be circumcised to be saved
Where to build the next church
Whether to allow women to pray
why: The apostles and elders concluded that Gentile believers did not need to follow the law of Moses to be saved by grace.

q: According to James, what is faith without works?
why: James teaches that genuine faith naturally shows itself in action, not as a way to earn salvation but as evidence of a living faith.

q: According to 1 Corinthians 13, what are two qualities of love?
why: Paul's famous description of love calls believers to a patient, kind, and selfless love that outlasts every other gift.

q: According to Romans 3, how many people have sinned?
why: Paul declares that every person has sinned and fallen short of God's glory, which is why everyone needs the grace found in Christ.

q: According to Ephesians 2, how are believers saved?
choices: By grace through faith, not of works
By being born into the right family
why: Salvation is a gift from God received by faith, not something earned by human effort, so no one can boast in themselves.

q: What does Paul say he can do through Christ who strengthens him?
why: Paul wrote this after describing contentment in both plenty and need, trusting Christ's strength in every circumstance.

q: How does Hebrews 11 define faith?
choices: Assurance of things hoped for, proof of things unseen
why: Hebrews opens its famous chapter on faith by describing it as confident trust in what God has promised, even before we see it.

q: In Revelation, what title does God give himself meaning 'the beginning and the end'?
why: Using the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet, God declares himself the beginning and end of all things.

q: What does John see at the very end of Revelation, replacing the first heaven and earth?
choices: A new heaven and a new earth
why: John's vision closes Scripture with the promise of a renewed creation where God dwells with his people forever.

q: What did God ask Abraham to count as a picture of his future offspring?
why: God took Abram outside to look at the stars, promising descendants beyond counting, a promise Abraham believed by faith.

q: What happened to Lot's wife when she looked back at Sodom?
choices: She became a pillar of salt
She was swallowed by the earth
why: Despite being warned not to look back, Lot's wife did, and she became a pillar of salt, a sobering warning about lingering after judgment.

q: Why did God confuse the languages at the Tower of Babel?
choices: To scatter people who were building a tower to make a name for themselves
why: The people tried to build a tower to make themselves great, so God confused their language and scattered them across the earth.

q: Who was the first murderer in the Bible, killing his own brother?
why: Jealous that his offering was rejected while his brother's was accepted, Cain killed Abel in a field, the Bible's first murder.

q: What did Esau trade his birthright for?
why: Overwhelmed by hunger, Esau despised his birthright and traded it away for a simple meal of bread and stew.

q: What miraculous sign confirmed Aaron as God's chosen priest?
choices: His staff budded and bore almonds
why: Among all twelve tribal staffs left overnight, only Aaron's sprouted, budded, blossomed, and bore ripe almonds, showing God's choice clearly.

q: What is the Shema, the central declaration in Deuteronomy 6?
choices: Hear, Israel, Yahweh is our God, Yahweh is one
I am the Alpha and Omega
The Lord bless you and keep you
why: The Shema calls Israel to love God with all their heart, soul, and strength, and it remains central to Jewish worship today.

q: Who called out to young Samuel in the night, though Samuel first thought it was Eli?
why: God called Samuel by name in the night, and once Eli realized what was happening, he told Samuel how to respond to the Lord.

q: How did God speak to Elijah at Horeb after the wind, earthquake, and fire?
why: God was not in the dramatic wind, earthquake, or fire, but spoke to discouraged Elijah in a gentle, quiet voice.

q: What happened to Saul's sight immediately after Ananias prayed for him?
choices: Something like scales fell from his eyes
He saw a vision of heaven
why: Scales fell from Saul's eyes and his sight was restored, and he was baptized and filled with the Holy Spirit that same day.

q: What did Barnabas do with a field he owned to help the early church?
choices: Sold it and gave the money to the apostles
why: Barnabas, whose name means 'son of encouragement,' generously sold his field and laid the proceeds at the apostles' feet.

q: Who were set apart by the Holy Spirit at Antioch to be sent out as missionaries?
why: While the believers at Antioch worshiped and fasted, the Spirit set apart Barnabas and Saul for the missionary work God had for them.

q: According to 1 John, what simple statement describes God's nature?
why: John teaches that love isn't just something God does, but who God fundamentally is, and that truth shapes how believers are called to love others.

q: According to 2 Timothy, what is all Scripture described as?
why: Paul tells Timothy that Scripture is God-breathed, equipping believers for teaching, correction, and every good work.

q: In Philemon, for whom does Paul appeal to his friend Philemon?
why: Paul asks Philemon to receive Onesimus back not merely as a slave but as a beloved brother in Christ.

q: According to Colossians, what role does Christ hold over the church?
why: Paul describes Christ as the head of the church, the beginning and firstborn from the dead, holding first place in everything.

q: According to 1 Thessalonians 4, what will happen when the Lord descends with a shout and a trumpet call?
choices: The dead in Christ will rise first
Only the living will be caught up
why: Paul comforts believers grieving the dead by describing how the Lord's return will bring resurrection and reunion for all who believe.

q: To how many churches is the book of Revelation addressed at its opening?
why: John writes to seven churches in Asia -- Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamum, Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, and Laodicea -- each receiving a specific message.

q: How many elders does John see seated around God's throne in Revelation?
why: Twenty-four elders in white with golden crowns surround the throne, worshiping continually before God.

q: What is the number associated with the beast in Revelation?
why: John calls readers to use wisdom in calculating this number, a symbol of a human power that opposes God.

q: How many years of plenty did Joseph predict for Egypt before famine came?
why: Joseph interpreted Pharaoh's dreams to mean seven years of abundance followed by seven years of famine, wisdom that saved many lives.

q: What did the Israelites put on their doorposts to be spared during the final plague?
why: The blood of the Passover lamb on the doorposts caused the plague to pass over Israelite homes, sparing their firstborn.

q: On the Day of Atonement, what happened to the goat chosen by lot as the 'scapegoat'?
choices: It was sacrificed on the altar
It carried the people's sins away into the wilderness
It was kept as a pet
It was given to the priest
why: The scapegoat symbolically carried Israel's sins away from the camp, pointing forward to how Christ would fully remove sin.

q: Who did God appoint as Moses' successor to lead Israel into the promised land?
why: After Moses died, God commissioned Joshua to lead the people across the Jordan into the land God had promised.

q: What does Ecclesiastes say there is a time for?
why: The Preacher observes that life has a season for everything -- a time to weep and a time to laugh, to plant and to harvest.

q: What phrase does the Preacher repeat at the start of Ecclesiastes to describe fleeting life apart from God?
why: The Preacher's refrain reminds readers that chasing wealth, pleasure, and achievement apart from God ultimately leaves us empty.

q: According to Proverbs, in what should we trust rather than our own understanding?
why: Proverbs calls believers to trust God fully rather than leaning only on their own limited understanding of a situation.

q: Job declares confidently that his what 'lives' even in the midst of his suffering?
why: Even stripped of everything, Job clung to the certain hope that his Redeemer lives and would one day stand upon the earth.

q: Who tested Job, though only within limits God set?
why: God permitted Satan to test Job's faith but set a clear limit on what could be done, showing God's sovereignty even in Job's suffering.

q: Which psalm captures the exiles' grief, beginning 'By the rivers of Babylon'?
why: This psalm expresses the deep sorrow of God's people exiled far from Jerusalem, longing for home.

q: Which psalm is David's prayer of repentance after his sin with Bathsheba?
why: After the prophet Nathan confronted him, David poured out heartfelt repentance in this psalm, asking God to create in him a clean heart.

q: What is the shortest verse in the Bible, found in the account of Lazarus?
why: At the tomb of his friend Lazarus, Jesus was moved to tears -- a brief but profound verse showing his deep compassion.
